title: Butterscotch
subtitle: Old-fashioned hard-ball toffee with butter and demerara sugar
excerpt: Classic butterscotch made with demerara sugar, butter, and a touch of golden syrup, cooked to hard-ball stage at 127°C and poured into a tin to set. Cut or broken into pieces. Makes about four hundred grams.
sourceNotes: Butterscotch is documented in British domestic confectionery from the early nineteenth century, traditionally associated with Doncaster where it was made commercially from the 1820s. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: butterscotch is recorded as a demerara sugar, butter, and golden syrup confection cooked to a firm setting stage. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Doncaster butterscotch made with brown sugar and butter is recorded in the confectionery chapter with the note that the toffee should set to a firm, glassy snap and not be soft or chewy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 3 weeks. Keep dry; moisture softens the toffee.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: hard-ball stage, 127°C; a drop in cold water forms a firm but pliable ball

glossaryTerms.definition: A sugar cooking stage reached at 121–130°C. A small amount dropped into cold water forms a firm ball that holds its shape but can be deformed with effort. This is the target stage for butterscotch, nougat, and firm caramels. At this point the sugar has reduced significantly but is not yet brittle. The distinction between hard-ball and hard-crack (150°C) matters to the finished texture: hard-ball produces a chewy-to-firm sweet; hard-crack produces a glassy, snapping sweet.

content.text: at 127°C, which produces a firm, glassy toffee that shatters cleanly rather than cheeping. The demerara sugar gives the butterscotch its amber colour and its characteristic caramel-molasses flavour. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Line a 20 × 20 cm tin with lightly oiled baking paper. Put the {{demerara-sugar}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{golden-syrup}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 75 ml cold water in a medium saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar and butter have melted. Clip the sugar thermometer to the side with the tip well clear of the base. Raise the heat to medium and bring to a boil without stirring. Cook until the thermometer reads 127°C; about 15–18 minutes. The toffee will be amber-gold. Pour immediately into the prepared tin; do not scrape the pan. Leave to cool and set at room temperature for at least 1 hour, then lift out using the paper and break or cut into pieces.
